      SPAC PIPE insider trading
   =20
 =20

A special purpose acquisition company is a publicly traded shell company th=
at raises a pot of money to go out and find a private company, merge with i=
t, and take it public. When the SPAC goes public, it sells shares for $10 e=
ach and puts the money in the pot; then the shares trade=C2=A0on the stock =
exchange while it looks for a target. Initially, they=C2=A0should trade at =
roughly $10, since they represent a claim on $10 in the pot of cash. But th=
en the SPAC finds a deal, negotiates it, signs it, and announces it to the =
market, and then the shares of the SPAC will trade to reflect the market=E2=
=80=99s view=C2=A0of the deal. If the deal is good, the SPAC shares will tr=
ade up, to $12 or $15 or $35 or whatever. If the deal is bad, the SPAC shar=
es will probably trade at about $10, since SPAC investors who don=E2=80=99t=
 like the deal can get their money back instead of rolling their shares int=
o the new company.
That feature of SPACs =E2=80=94=C2=A0that investors who don=E2=80=99t like =
the deal can get their money back =E2=80=94=C2=A0is a little annoying for t=
he target company. If you are a private company and you sign a deal with a =
$200 million SPAC to go public, you are hoping to get the SPAC=E2=80=99s $2=
00 million: Like an initial public offering, a SPAC merger is not just a wa=
y to get a stock-exchange listing but also a way to raise money. But the SP=
AC=E2=80=99s shareholders can take their money back, so you don=E2=80=99t a=
ctually know, when you sign the deal, how much money you will get. Could be=
 $200 million, could be $10 million, could be zero.
There is a standard way to address this problem.
  [1]
 This is the PIPE, the private investment in public equity: When the SPAC f=
inds a target and negotiates a deal, the SPAC sponsor and the target and th=
eir bankers will go out and find a few big investors who commit to invest i=
n the deal alongside the SPAC. So a $200 million SPAC deal might come with =
a $150 million PIPE, where a handful of institutional investors agree to in=
vest $150 million in the target. This has two advantages. One is that the b=
ig institutions are validating the price of the deal: If a big investor is =
willing to invest $150 million of their own money in the deal, then that=E2=
=80=99s a good sign to the SPAC=E2=80=99s investors that they should also s=
tay in the SPAC and not withdraw their money. The other advantage is that, =
even if the public SPAC investors all=C2=A0do=C2=A0withdraw their money, at=
 least the target gets something: The PIPE investors are generally committe=
d to the deal and don=E2=80=99t have withdrawal rights, so if you sign a $2=
00 million SPAC deal with a $150 million PIPE, the target company can count=
 on getting a public listing and at least $150 million.
  [2]

Mechanically, this means that when a SPAC and a target company discuss a me=
rger, and get pretty far along and decide to actually do it, but=C2=A0befor=
e=C2=A0they announce it publicly, they will call up some big investors and =
say =E2=80=9Chey do you want in on the PIPE for this deal?=E2=80=9D And the=
 big investors will have to think about it and decide if it=E2=80=99s a goo=
d deal and then get back to the SPAC and target with a yes or a no. And mea=
nwhile the SPAC=E2=80=99s stock is trading in the public markets, and most =
of the people trading the stock don=E2=80=99t know about the deal. But if y=
ou=E2=80=99re an investor who got the call about the PIPE, you do.
And so if you think that the SPAC=E2=80=99s stock will trade=C2=A0up=C2=A0w=
hen the deal is announced, you can go buy the stock at $10, and wait for th=
e deal to be announced, and then it will trade up to $15 and you will make =
a quick profit.
Oh I mean you shouldn=E2=80=99t do that; it=E2=80=99s super illegal. It=E2=
=80=99s obviously insider trading.=C2=A0When the SPAC or its bankers call y=
ou up to ask about the PIPE,  they will =E2=80=9Cwall-cross=E2=80=9D you: T=
hey=E2=80=99ll start the conversation by saying something like =E2=80=9CWe =
would like to ask you about a SPAC PIPE deal, but we can=E2=80=99t tell you=
 what the SPAC is or what the target is until you agree to keep that inform=
ation confidential and not trade on it until it is announced.=E2=80=9D This=
 is all very standard and orchestrated, and you will say =E2=80=9Cokay I pr=
omise not to trade,=E2=80=9D and then they=E2=80=99ll tell you, and then if=
 you trade anyway you will get in trouble.
  [3]

Not everyone in and around the SPAC business is entirely committed to follo=
wing the letter and the spirit of the law, though, and you=E2=80=99d kind o=
f expect there to be a lot of insider trading around SPAC deals, as investo=
rs get wall-crossed on the PIPEs and then put on their own bets. And late l=
ast month the US Securities and Exchange Commission and federal prosecutors=
=C2=A0brought a case:

The Securities and Exchange Commission ... filed insider trading charges ag=
ainst Sean Wygovsky, a former trader at a Canadian asset management firm, a=
nd Christopher Matthaei, a former partner at a U.S. broker-dealer, for usin=
g nonpublic information in advance of at least seven merger announcements i=
nvolving Special Purpose Acquisition Companies (SPACs) to earn illicit prof=
its of more than $3.4 million.
The SEC=E2=80=99s complaint alleges that Wygovsky learned material non-publ=
ic information about upcoming SPAC mergers from his employer=E2=80=99s invo=
lvement in transactions related to the mergers. The complaint further alleg=
es that, from May 2020 through April 2021, Wygovsky used encrypted messagin=
g to tip his close friend and trading client, Matthaei, about the upcoming =
mergers. According to the complaint, Matthaei, who ran a trading and resear=
ch group focused on SPACs during the relevant period, allegedly traded on W=
ygovsky=E2=80=99s tips.

Wygovsky worked at Polar Asset Management Partners, which would take wall-c=
rosses on SPAC PIPEs and put the relevant SPACs on its restricted list, for=
bidding employees from trading on them. This let Wygovsky know what SPAC de=
als were coming, so he allegedly found out more about them and tipped Matth=
aei, who then allegedly traded in his personal account and paid Wygovsky ba=
ck with luxury vacations:

On May 28, 2020, at 4:08 p.m., a colleague at the Asset Manager sent Wygovs=
ky an email stating: =E2=80=9CWe are over the wall on a SPAC deal. =E2=80=
=A6 The SPAC is SHLL, and is contemplating a merger with Hyliion =E2=80=93 =
it has a lot of similarities to the Nikola deal (VTIQ).=E2=80=9D The collea=
gue provided additional nonpublic details concerning Hyliion and the propos=
ed merger and PIPE terms, noting that the parties planned to announce the m=
erger during the week of June 15, 2020.
After receiving this material nonpublic information, Wygovsky tipped Mattha=
ei through communications on Telegram about the merger between Tortoise and=
 Hyliion for the purpose of Matthaei trading based on that information. =E2=
=80=A6

We  talked last month about Bed Bath & Beyond Inc.=E2=80=99s corporate fina=
ncing strategy. A few months ago, Bed Bath=E2=80=99s stock was trading at a=
bout $3, and it was about to file for bankruptcy, which would almost certai=
nly cause the stock to jump to $0 and stay there. (There is not enough mone=
y to pay creditors, and surely nothing left over for shareholders.) That wo=
uld be a sad outcome, and Bed Bath tried something different: It decided to=
 sell as much stock as possible to try to raise money to put off bankruptcy=
. Maybe it would work, the stock would rally, they=E2=80=99d sell a bunch o=
f stock, they=E2=80=99d pay off their debt and everything would be great. O=
r maybe it would fail, the stock would keep declining, they=E2=80=99d sell =
a bunch of stock anyway, and they=E2=80=99d raise some cash to give the cre=
ditors a slightly better recovery in bankruptcy.=C2=A0
Bed Bath started on this plan in February, and by the end of March (1) it h=
ad sold about 311 million shares (almost three times as many as it had outs=
tanding in January), (2) it had raised about $360 million and (3) it had dr=
iven its stock price down to $0.80. Fine, good, great work. Bad for the sha=
re price ($0.80 is less than $3), in a sense,=C2=A0but better than the alte=
rnative of bankruptcy ($0.80 is more than $0). We talked about this in Marc=
h because Bed Bath was slightly shifting the=C2=A0form=C2=A0of this plan =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0it=C2=A0had been selling the stock in a   complicated expens=
ive way to an investor called Hudson Bay Capital, which then sold it to Bed=
 Bath=E2=80=99s retail investors, and in March it stopped that and started =
selling the stock to those retail investors in a more straightforward way
  [4]
=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0but it was carrying on with the plan. It was hoping to=
 raise another $300 million, and warned that, if it couldn=E2=80=99t, =E2=
=80=9Cwe expect that we will likely file for bankruptcy protection, in whic=
h case holders of our common stock will likely receive no recovery at all f=
or the securities offered by this registration statement.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0
How are things going? Well, the stock closed yesterday at $0.3138, which is=
 lower than $0.80 and higher than zero and feels about right. The Wall Stre=
et Journal checked in with the people buying the shares:=C2=A0

Kais Maalej, a 49-year-old Uber driver and amateur investor active on socia=
l media, is a proponent of the theory on Reddit forums that distressed reta=
iler Bed Bath & Beyond Inc. will soon be acquired by a holding company asso=
ciated with billionaire Ryan Cohen.
=E2=80=9CI am very confident=E2=80=9D that Bed Bath & Beyond will be bought=
, said Mr. Maalej, who grew up in Tunisia and now lives in Queens. He said =
that he started buying Bed Bath & Beyond shares last year and has been buyi=
ng up more in recent months, including last week. =E2=80=9CI=E2=80=99m load=
ed with shares,=E2=80=9D he said.
It is this kind of confidence among individual investors that has kept trad=
ing volumes in Bed Bath & Beyond stock robust as the company floods the mar=
ket with hundreds of millions of shares even while warning about a potentia=
l bankruptcy.=C2=A0

Ahh. Yeesh. The Journal also checks in with the people who are effectively =
getting Maalej=E2=80=99s money: Bed Bath=E2=80=99s creditors.

Chris Rodriguez, 30, a professional investor in Southern California who man=
ages his own money, said he owns roughly $100,000 of the company=E2=80=99s =
unsecured bonds, which he purchased at around 9 or 10 cents on the dollar, =
betting some assets such as BuyBuy Baby can return some recovery in bankrup=
tcy.
He said that he thinks the chatter about a near-term acquisition by Mr. Coh=
en on social media is crazy and that the company has been taking advantage =
of these people to raise money on behalf of its lenders, though it likely w=
on=E2=80=99t be enough to avoid chapter 11.=C2=A0
=E2=80=9CI don=E2=80=99t know if there=E2=80=99s a lot of examples of compa=
nies diluting their shareholders right before bankruptcy,=E2=80=9D Mr. Rodr=
iguez said.

This is a minor quibble, but in meme-stock contexts people often abuse that=
 word, =E2=80=9Cdiluting.=E2=80=9D The problem here is not=C2=A0dilution. I=
f you owned Bed Bath shares six months ago, when they traded above $5, you =
are presumably=C2=A0sad=C2=A0that they are trading at 30 cents today, but y=
ou can=E2=80=99t really blame Bed Bath=E2=80=99s issuance of zillions of sh=
ares for that. If it hadn=E2=80=99t issued the shares, it would have just f=
iled for bankruptcy, and your shares would be worth zero. The fact that the=
re are more shares, and the fact that you now own a smaller percentage of t=
he company, is not the problem; the problem is that the company is running =
out of money. The dilution is great for you; it means that the company has =
(a bit) more money, and that you can sell your stock for 30 cents instead o=
f zero.
No, the problem is not that Bed Bath is diluting existing shareholders, it=
=E2=80=99s that it=E2=80=99s=C2=A0raising money from new shareholders=C2=A0=
in a quest that seems =E2=80=A6=C2=A0I mean, who am I to say =E2=80=9Chopel=
ess,=E2=80=9D and nothing here is ever investing advice, but =E2=80=A6=C2=
=A0quite risky, anyway? If you buy a share of Bed Bath today at $0.30, your=
 thirty cents is=C2=A0probably=C2=A0going to end up increasing some bondhol=
der=E2=80=99s recovery from like 9 cents on the dollar to, you know, 9.0000=
01 cents. The expected outcome of Bed Bath=E2=80=99s stock sale is that it =
is funneling money from unsophisticated retail investors to more-sophistica=
ted bondholders. It=E2=80=99s kind of gross! I don=E2=80=99t know:
Adam Pritchard, a securities law professor at the University of Michigan La=
w School, said there is no requirement in securities laws that the securiti=
es a company wants to sell are worth anything. =E2=80=9CThe premise is full=
 disclosure,=E2=80=9D Mr. Pritchard said. =E2=80=9CAs long as they=E2=80=99=
ve fully disclosed a very high risk of bankruptcy, what they=E2=80=99ve don=
e is legal.=E2=80=9D
I guess? When Hertz Global Holdings Inc. sold stock to meme-driven retail i=
nvestors=C2=A0while it was in bankruptcy in 2020, it very much disclosed th=
at it was in bankruptcy,  and even said that =E2=80=9Cwe expect that common=
 stock holders would not receive a recovery through any plan=E2=80=9D in ba=
nkruptcy. The US Securities and Exchange Commission quickly shut down the o=
ffering anyway. =E2=80=9CFraud in plain sight,=E2=80=9D one lawyer called i=
t: Hertz disclosed, in its SEC filings, that the stock was probably worthle=
ss, but its retail investors weren=E2=80=99t reading those filings or takin=
g those warnings seriously, and the SEC stepped in to stop them from hurtin=
g themselves.
In the event, Hertz=E2=80=99s business recovered, the equity had value,  th=
e retail investors were right and the SEC was wrong. I wrote:
The core=C2=A0idea of U.S. securities law is disclosure: Companies should a=
ccurately and completely disclose their situations to investors, and then i=
nvestors get to make up their own minds about what stocks to buy and how mu=
ch to pay. In weird and bubbly and meme-y times, that idea makes people ner=
vous: =E2=80=9CSure sure sure you have disclosed all your financial informa=
tion, but you are=C2=A0bankrupt, how can you sell stock to people? How can =
they buy it?=E2=80=9D And so there is a temptation to meddle, to restrict t=
rades even with full disclosure, to protect investors from their own fully =
informed but apparently crazy=C2=A0decisions. With Hertz, that happens to h=
ave been a mistake.
I suppose that gives Bed Bath a little more room to maneuver: It is temptin=
g for regulators to second-guess retail investors=E2=80=99 decisions to buy=
 shares of bankrupt-ish meme-stock companies, and the SEC succumbed to that=
 temptation with Hertz and was wrong. And now Bed Bath is warning of bankru=
ptcy and pounding out as much stock as its retail investors will buy, and t=
hey keep buying it.

oes that work? No, no it does not, for tons of reasons (it is somewhere bet=
ween hard and impossible for shareholders of most public companies to call =

s vote on the board and not the CEO directly, the CEO can=E2=80=99t just wi=

<table class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" cellp=
adding=3D"0">
<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=
-table-lspace: 0pt; mso-table-rspace: 0pt;">
<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">SPAC PIPE insider trading</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A special purpose acquisition company is a pub=
licly traded shell company that raises a pot of money to go out and find a =
private company, merge with it, and take it public. When the SPAC goes publ=
ic, it sells shares for $10 each and puts the money in the pot; then the sh=
ares trade=C2=A0on the stock exchange while it looks for a target. Initiall=
y, they=C2=A0should trade at roughly $10, since they represent a claim on $=
10 in the pot of cash. But then the SPAC finds a deal, negotiates it, signs=
 it, and announces it to the market, and then the shares of the SPAC will t=
rade to reflect the market=E2=80=99s view=C2=A0of the deal. If the deal is =
good, the SPAC shares will trade up, to $12 or $15 or <a href=3D"https://li=
nk.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31142814.258450/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21=
iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDItMjMvdGVzbGEtcml2YWwtbHVjaWQtbW=
90b3JzLXBhaWQtYS1wcmljZS1mb3ItaXRzLXNwYWMtZGVhbD9jbXBpZD1CQkQwNDEyMjNfTU9OR=
VlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0y=
MzA0MTImdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB872981b0" =
itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"c=
olor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; =
border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color:=
 #ccc;">$35</a> or whatever. If the deal is bad, the SPAC shares will proba=
bly trade at about $10, since SPAC investors who don=E2=80=99t like the dea=
l can get their money back instead of rolling their shares into the new com=
pany.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That feature of SPACs =E2=80=94=C2=A0that inve=
stors who don=E2=80=99t like the deal can get their money back =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0is a little annoying for the target company. If you are a private compan=
y and you sign a deal with a $200 million SPAC to go public, you are hoping=
 to get the SPAC=E2=80=99s $200 million: Like an initial public offering, a=
 SPAC merger is not just a way to get a stock-exchange listing but also a w=
ay to raise money. But the SPAC=E2=80=99s shareholders can take their money=
 back, so you don=E2=80=99t actually know, when you sign the deal, how much=
 money you will get. Could be $200 million, could be $10 million, could be =
zero.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There is a standard way to address this proble=
m.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1" style=3D"color: #000000;=
 text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-=
color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: tra=
nsparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a> This is the PIPE, the private investment in public equity: When the SP=
AC finds a target and negotiates a deal, the SPAC sponsor and the target an=
d their bankers will go out and find a few big investors who commit to inve=
st in the deal alongside the SPAC. So a $200 million SPAC deal might come w=
ith a $150 million PIPE, where a handful of institutional investors agree t=
o invest $150 million in the target. This has two advantages. One is that t=
he big institutions are validating the price of the deal: If a big investor=
 is willing to invest $150 million of their own money in the deal, then tha=
t=E2=80=99s a good sign to the SPAC=E2=80=99s investors that they should al=
so stay in the SPAC and not withdraw their money. The other advantage is th=
at, even if the public SPAC investors all=C2=A0<em>do=C2=A0</em>withdraw th=
eir money, at least the target gets something: The PIPE investors are gener=
ally committed to the deal and don=E2=80=99t have withdrawal rights, so if =
you sign a $200 million SPAC deal with a $150 million PIPE, the target comp=
any can count on getting a public listing and at least $150 million.<a clas=
s=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-2" style=3D"color: #000000; text-dec=
oration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #0=
00000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent =
!important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Mechanically, this means that when a SPAC and =
a target company discuss a merger, and get pretty far along and decide to a=
ctually do it, but=C2=A0<em>before=C2=A0</em>they announce it publicly, the=
y will call up some big investors and say =E2=80=9Chey do you want in on th=
e PIPE for this deal?=E2=80=9D And the big investors will have to think abo=
ut it and decide if it=E2=80=99s a good deal and then get back to the SPAC =
and target with a yes or a no. And meanwhile the SPAC=E2=80=99s stock is tr=
ading in the public markets, and most of the people trading the stock don=
=E2=80=99t know about the deal. But if you=E2=80=99re an investor who got t=
he call about the PIPE, you do.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And so if you think that the SPAC=E2=80=99s st=
ock will trade=C2=A0<em>up=C2=A0</em>when the deal is announced, you can go=
 buy the stock at $10, and wait for the deal to be announced, and then it w=
ill trade up to $15 and you will make a quick profit.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Oh I mean you <em>shouldn=E2=80=99t</em> do th=
at; it=E2=80=99s super illegal. It=E2=80=99s obviously insider trading.=C2=
=A0When the SPAC or its bankers call you up to ask about the PIPE, <a href=
=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31142814.258450/aHR0cHM6L=
y93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDQtMDYvd2FsbC1zdHJlZXQt=
ZW1icmFjZXMtd2lua3MtYW5kLW51ZGdlcy1hcy1lcXVpdHktbWFya2V0LWZhbGxzP2NtcGlkPUJ=
CRDA0MTIyM19NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dG=
VyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIzMDQxMiZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a3=
19a23cB61af3f82" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_=
blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bo=
ttom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; =
background-color: #ccc;"> they will =E2=80=9Cwall-cross=E2=80=9D you</a>: T=
hey=E2=80=99ll start the conversation by saying something like =E2=80=9CWe =
would like to ask you about a SPAC PIPE deal, but we can=E2=80=99t tell you=
 what the SPAC is or what the target is until you agree to keep that inform=
ation confidential and not trade on it until it is announced.=E2=80=9D This=
 is all very standard and orchestrated, and you will say =E2=80=9Cokay I pr=
omise not to trade,=E2=80=9D and then they=E2=80=99ll tell you, and then if=
 you trade anyway you will get in trouble.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=
=3D"#footnote-3" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important;=
 border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-styl=
e: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inli=
ne-block;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Not everyone in and around the SPAC business i=
s entirely committed to following the letter and the spirit of the law, tho=
ugh, and you=E2=80=99d kind of expect there to be a lot of insider trading =
around SPAC deals, as investors get wall-crossed on the PIPEs and then put =
on their own bets. And late last month the <a href=3D"https://link.mail.blo=
ombergbusiness.com/click/31142814.258450/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9uZXdzL3=
ByZXNzLXJlbGVhc2UvMjAyMy02Ng/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB88f694ca" itemprop=3D=
"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0000=
00; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bott=
om-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">US =
Securities and Exchange Commission</a> and <a href=3D"https://link.mail.blo=
ombergbusiness.com/click/31142814.258450/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuanVzdGljZS5nb3YvdX=
Nhby1uai9wci9mb3JtZXItcGFydG5lci1icm9rZXItZGVhbGVyLWZpcm0tY2hhcmdlZC0zNC1ta=
WxsaW9uLWluc2lkZXItdHJhZGluZy1zY2hlbWU/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB7e88f7b3" i=
temprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"co=
lor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; b=
order-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: =
#ccc;">federal prosecutors</a>=C2=A0brought a case:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The Securities and Exchange Commission ... fil=
ed insider trading charges against Sean Wygovsky, a former trader at a Cana=
dian asset management firm, and Christopher Matthaei, a former partner at a=
 U.S. broker-dealer, for using nonpublic information in advance of at least=
 seven merger announcements involving Special Purpose Acquisition Companies=
 (SPACs) to earn illicit profits of more than $3.4 million.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The SEC=E2=80=99s complaint alleges that Wygov=
sky learned material non-public information about upcoming SPAC mergers fro=
m his employer=E2=80=99s involvement in transactions related to the mergers=
. The complaint further alleges that, from May 2020 through April 2021, Wyg=
ovsky used encrypted messaging to tip his close friend and trading client, =
Matthaei, about the upcoming mergers. According to the complaint, Matthaei,=
 who ran a trading and research group focused on SPACs during the relevant =
period, allegedly traded on Wygovsky=E2=80=99s tips.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Wygovsky <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomber=
gbusiness.com/click/31142814.258450/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXd=
zL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDctMDIvY2FuYWRpYW4tdHJhZGVyLWNoYXJnZWQtd2l0aC1pbnNpZG=
VyLXRyYWRpbmctaW4tbmV3LXlvcms_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDQxMjIzX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZ=
Gl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjMwNDEyJnV0bV9jYW1w=
YWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB5fe7fd8a" itemprop=3D"StoryLi=
nk" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text=
-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color=
: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">worked at P=
olar Asset Management Partners</a>, which would take wall-crosses on SPAC P=
IPEs and put the relevant SPACs on its restricted list, forbidding employee=
s from trading on them. This let Wygovsky know what SPAC deals were coming,=
 so he allegedly found out more about them and tipped Matthaei, who then al=
legedly traded in his personal account and paid Wygovsky back <a href=3D"ht=
tps://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31142814.258450/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3c=
uc2VjLmdvdi9saXRpZ2F0aW9uL2NvbXBsYWludHMvMjAyMy9jb21wLXByMjAyMy02Ni5wZGY/60=
7f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB9b50c1ec" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemsc=
ope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impo=
rtant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-botto=
m-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">with luxury vacations</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On May 28, 2020, at 4:08 p.m., a colleague at =
the Asset Manager sent Wygovsky an email stating: =E2=80=9CWe are over the =
wall on a SPAC deal. =E2=80=A6 The SPAC is SHLL, and is contemplating a mer=
ger with Hyliion =E2=80=93 it has a lot of similarities to the Nikola deal =
(VTIQ).=E2=80=9D The colleague provided additional nonpublic details concer=
ning Hyliion and the proposed merger and PIPE terms, noting that the partie=
s planned to announce the merger during the week of June 15, 2020.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">After receiving this material nonpublic inform=
ation, Wygovsky tipped Matthaei through communications on Telegram about th=
e merger between Tortoise and Hyliion for the purpose of Matthaei trading b=
ased on that information. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On May 29, 2020, at 7:09 a.m., based on the ma=
terial nonpublic information Wygovsky provided, Matthaei began entering mul=
tiple buy orders for Tortoise common stock and warrants in his brokerage ac=
counts with Broker 1 and Broker 2. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On June 19, 2020, Tortoise and Hyliion announc=
ed a merger agreement, and the prices of Tortoise common stock and warrants=
 closed up 37.4% and 181.3%, respectively, from the previous day=E2=80=99s =
closing price.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In total, Matthaei=E2=80=99s long position in =
Tortoise securities generated illicit profits of approximately $1,489,026.<=
/p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On June 27, 2020, following the Tortoise merge=
r announcement, Wygovsky and Matthaei travelled together with their familie=
s to Saint Barth=C3=A9lemy in the Caribbean. Matthaei paid a total of appro=
ximately $41,950 for the group to travel by private jet, and approximately =
$76,050 for both families to stay at a luxury private villa.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On August 1, 2020, Matthaei paid approximately=
 $38,500 for Wygovsky and his family to travel from Saint Barth=C3=A9lemy t=
o Nantucket using a private jet.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The classic way for insider trading to work is=
 that there is an insider with the information who cannot trade on it becau=
se it would be too obvious, so he gives the information to his friend, the =
friend trades on it, and then the friend pays the insider back for the info=
rmation. The most traditional=C2=A0way for the friend to pay back the insid=
er is by <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31142814.=
258450/aHR0cHM6Ly9hcmNoaXZlLm55dGltZXMuY29tL2RlYWxib29rLm55dGltZXMuY29tLzIw=
MTMvMDQvMTEvZm9ybWVyLWtwbWctcGFydG5lci1pcy1jaGFyZ2VkLXdpdGgtaW5zaWRlci10cmF=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusin=
ess.com/click/31142814.258450/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL=

" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !importa=
nt; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-s=
tyle: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> talked last month</a> about Bed Bath=
 & Beyond Inc.=E2=80=99s corporate financing strategy. A few months ago=
, Bed Bath=E2=80=99s stock was trading at about $3, and it was about to fil=
e for bankruptcy, which would almost certainly cause the stock to jump to $=
0 and stay there. (There is not enough money to pay creditors, and surely n=
othing left over for shareholders.) That would be a sad outcome, and Bed Ba=
th tried something different: It decided to sell as much stock as possible =
to try to raise money to put off bankruptcy. Maybe it would work, the stock=
 would rally, they=E2=80=99d sell a bunch of stock, they=E2=80=99d pay off =
their debt and everything would be great. Or maybe it would fail, the stock=
 would keep declining, they=E2=80=99d sell a bunch of stock anyway, and the=
y=E2=80=99d raise some cash to give the creditors a slightly better recover=
y in bankruptcy.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Bed Bath started on this plan in February, and=
 by the end of March (1) it had sold about 311 million shares (almost three=
 times as many as it had outstanding in January), (2) it had raised about $=
360 million and (3) it had driven its stock price down to $0.80. Fine, good=
, great work. Bad for the share price ($0.80 is less than $3), in a sense,=
=C2=A0but better than the alternative of bankruptcy ($0.80 is more than $0)=
. We talked about this in March because Bed Bath was slightly shifting the=
=C2=A0<em>form=C2=A0</em>of this plan =E2=80=94=C2=A0it=C2=A0<em>had </em>b=
een selling the stock in a <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.c=
om/click/31142814.258450/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydG=
ljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDItMDcvYmVkLWJhdGgtYmV5b25kLXNvbGQtc29tZS1zdG9jaz9jbXBpZD1CQ=
kQwNDEyMjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRl=
ciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzA0MTImdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a31=
9a23cB2c70f8b1" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_b=
lank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bot=
tom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; b=
ackground-color: #ccc;">  complicated expensive way</a> to an investor call=
ed Hudson Bay Capital, which then sold it to Bed Bath=E2=80=99s retail inve=
stors, and in March it stopped that and started selling the stock to those =
retail investors in a more straightforward way<a class=3D"footnote-index" h=
ref=3D"#footnote-4" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !importa=
nt; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-s=
tyle: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: i=
nline-block;">
<span>[4]</span>
</a>=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0but it was carrying on with the plan. It was hopin=
g to raise another $300 million, and <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/31142814.258450/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9l=
ZGdhci9kYXRhLzg4NjE1OC8wMDAxMTkzMTI1MjMwODQ2OTMvZDQ2ODgyM2Q0MjRiNS5odG0/607=
f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB55c9339e" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemsco=
pe" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impor=
tant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom=
-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">warned that</a>, if it couldn=E2=80=
=99t, =E2=80=9Cwe expect that we will likely file for bankruptcy protection=
, in which case holders of our common stock will likely receive no recovery=
 at all for the securities offered by this registration statement.=E2=80=9D=
=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">How are things going? Well, the stock closed y=
esterday at $0.3138, which is lower than $0.80 and higher than zero and fee=
ls about right. The Wall Street Journal <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomb=
ergbusiness.com/click/31142814.258450/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9hcnRpY2xlc=
y9iZWQtYmF0aC1iZXlvbmRzLXN0b2NrLXNhbGUtZGVsdWdlLWx1cmVzLXJldGFpbC1pbnZlc3Rv=
cnMtYmI5ZGIxYzU_bW9kPWl0cF93c2omcnU9eWFob28/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB3d6dd3=
82" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;">checked in with the people buying the shares</a>:=C2=A0</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Kais Maalej, a 49-year-old Uber driver and ama=
teur investor active on social media, is a proponent of the theory on Reddi=
t forums that distressed retailer Bed Bath & Beyond Inc. will soon be a=
cquired by a holding company associated with billionaire Ryan Cohen.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CI am very confident=E2=80=9D that Bed=
 Bath & Beyond will be bought, said Mr. Maalej, who grew up in Tunisia =
and now lives in Queens. He said that he started buying Bed Bath & Beyo=
nd shares last year and has been buying up more in recent months, including=
 last week. =E2=80=9CI=E2=80=99m loaded with shares,=E2=80=9D he said.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It is this kind of confidence among individual=
 investors that has kept trading volumes in Bed Bath & Beyond stock rob=
ust as the company floods the market with hundreds of millions of shares ev=
en while warning about a potential bankruptcy.=C2=A0</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Ahh. Yeesh. The Journal also checks in with th=
e people who are effectively getting Maalej=E2=80=99s money: Bed Bath=E2=80=
=99s creditors.</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Chris Rodriguez, 30, a professional investor i=
n Southern California who manages his own money, said he owns roughly $100,=
000 of the company=E2=80=99s unsecured bonds, which he purchased at around =
9 or 10 cents on the dollar, betting some assets such as BuyBuy Baby can re=
turn some recovery in bankruptcy.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">He said that he thinks the chatter about a nea=
r-term acquisition by Mr. Cohen on social media is crazy and that the compa=
ny has been taking advantage of these people to raise money on behalf of it=
s lenders, though it likely won=E2=80=99t be enough to avoid chapter 11.=C2=
=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CI don=E2=80=99t know if there=E2=80=
=99s a lot of examples of companies diluting their shareholders right befor=
e bankruptcy,=E2=80=9D Mr. Rodriguez said.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This is a minor quibble, but in meme-stock con=
texts people often abuse that word, =E2=80=9Cdiluting.=E2=80=9D The problem=
 here is not=C2=A0<em>dilution</em>. If you owned Bed Bath shares six month=
s ago, when they traded above $5, you are presumably=C2=A0<em>sad=C2=A0</em=
>that they are trading at 30 cents today, but you can=E2=80=99t really blam=
e Bed Bath=E2=80=99s issuance of zillions of shares for that. If it hadn=E2=
=80=99t issued the shares, it would have just filed for bankruptcy, and you=
r shares would be worth zero. The fact that there are more shares, and the =
fact that you now own a smaller percentage of the company, is not the probl=
em; the problem is that the company is running out of money. The dilution i=
s great for you; it means that the company has (a bit) more money, and that=
 you can sell your stock for 30 cents instead of zero.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">No, the problem is not that Bed Bath is diluti=
ng existing shareholders, it=E2=80=99s that it=E2=80=99s=C2=A0<em>raising m=
oney from new shareholders=C2=A0</em>in a quest that seems =E2=80=A6=C2=A0I=
 mean, who am I to say =E2=80=9Chopeless,=E2=80=9D and nothing here is ever=
 investing advice, but =E2=80=A6=C2=A0quite risky, anyway? If you buy a sha=
re of Bed Bath today at $0.30, your thirty cents is=C2=A0<em>probably=C2=A0=
</em>going to end up increasing some bondholder=E2=80=99s recovery from lik=
e 9 cents on the dollar to, you know, 9.000001 cents. The <em>expected </em=
>outcome of Bed Bath=E2=80=99s stock sale is that it is funneling money fro=
m unsophisticated retail investors to more-sophisticated bondholders. It=E2=
=80=99s kind of gross! I don=E2=80=99t know:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Adam Pritchard, a securities law p=
rofessor at the University of Michigan Law School, said there is no require=
ment in securities laws that the securities a company wants to sell are wor=
th anything. =E2=80=9CThe premise is full disclosure,=E2=80=9D Mr. Pritchar=
d said. =E2=80=9CAs long as they=E2=80=99ve fully disclosed a very high ris=
k of bankruptcy, what they=E2=80=99ve done is legal.=E2=80=9D</p></blockquo=
te>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I guess? When Hertz Global Holdings Inc. sold =
stock to meme-driven retail investors=C2=A0<em>while it was in bankruptcy <=
/em>in 2020, it very much disclosed that it was in bankruptcy, <a href=3D"h=
ttps://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31142814.258450/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3=
cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjAtMDYtMjIvdGhlLWJlc3QtZnJhd=
WQtaXMtaW4tcGxhaW4tc2lnaHQ_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDQxMjIzX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1=
bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjMwNDEyJnV0bV9jYW1wYWl=
nbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB88f0c2d6" itemprop=3D"StoryLink"=
 itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-de=
coration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #=
000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> and even said=
</a> that =E2=80=9Cwe expect that common stock holders would not receive a =
recovery through any plan=E2=80=9D in bankruptcy. The US Securities and Exc=
hange Commission quickly shut down the offering anyway. =E2=80=9CFraud in p=
lain sight,=E2=80=9D one lawyer called it: Hertz disclosed, in its SEC fili=
ngs, that the stock was probably worthless, but its retail investors weren=
=E2=80=99t reading those filings or taking those warnings seriously, and th=
e SEC stepped in to stop them from hurting themselves.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In the event, Hertz=E2=80=99s business recover=
ed, the equity had value, <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.co=
m/click/31142814.258450/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGl=
jbGVzLzIwMjEtMDUtMTIvdGhlLWhlcnR6LWdhbWJsZXJzLXdlcmUtcmlnaHQ_Y21waWQ9QkJEMD=
QxMjIzX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImd=
XRtX3Rlcm09MjMwNDEyJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23=
cBcfa3de69" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank=
" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-=
width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backg=
round-color: #ccc;"> the retail investors were right and the SEC was wrong<=
/a>. I wrote:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The core=C2=A0idea of U.S. securit=
ies law is disclosure: Companies should accurately and completely disclose =
their situations to investors, and then investors get to make up their own =
minds about what stocks to buy and how much to pay. In weird and bubbly and=
 meme-y times, that idea makes people nervous: =E2=80=9CSure sure sure you =
have disclosed all your financial information, but you are=C2=A0<em>bankrup=
t</em>, how can you sell stock to people? How can they buy it?=E2=80=9D And=
 so there is a temptation to meddle, to restrict trades even with full disc=
losure, to protect investors from their own fully informed but apparently c=
razy=C2=A0decisions. With Hertz, that happens to have been a mistake.</p></=
blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I suppose that gives Bed Bath a little more ro=
om to maneuver: It is tempting for regulators to second-guess retail invest=
ors=E2=80=99 decisions to buy shares of bankrupt-ish meme-stock companies, =
and the SEC succumbed to that temptation with Hertz and was wrong. And now =
Bed Bath is warning of bankruptcy and pounding out as much stock as its ret=
ail investors will buy, and they keep buying it.</p>

atever</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Twitter Inc. has ceased to be an independent c=
ompany after merging with a newly formed shell firm called X Corp., driving=
 speculation about what Elon Musk intends for the social media platform.</p=
>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Twitter =E2=80=9Cno longer exists=E2=80=9D aft=
er being merged with X Corp., according to an April 4 document submitted in=

chief executive officer, Jack Dorsey, last year by conservative activist La=
ura Loomer.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It=E2=80=99s unclear what the change means for=

for $44 billion last year. The billionaire owner has in the past suggested =

 =E2=80=94 which he dubbed an =E2=80=9Ceverything app.=E2=80=9D Musk tweete=
d about the move Tuesday with the single character =E2=80=9CX.=E2=80=9D</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It would be very funny if Twitter=E2=80=99s la=
wyers were responding to lawsuits against Twitter by saying =E2=80=9Cah sor=
ry, Twitter doesn=E2=80=99t exist anymore, you can=E2=80=99t sue us,=E2=80=
=9D but of course that=E2=80=99s not what=E2=80=99s happening; they=E2=80=
=99re <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31142814.258=

er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;"> saying more like</a> =E2=80=9Cwe=E2=80=99ve =
changed our name to X so now you are suing X.=E2=80=9D One possible reading=
 of this, which I guess Musk has encouraged, is that X is to Twitter what A=
lphabet is to Google, a sign that Musk=E2=80=99s intentions for the company=
 go far beyond the existing social media site and extend to, uh, payments m=
ostly:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Musk said during a Twitter Spaces =
interview with the BBC late Tuesday at the company=E2=80=99s San Francisco =
headquarters that he had plans for the new holding company X, reaffirming t=
hat Twitter was =E2=80=9Can accelerant=E2=80=9D toward his vision of an eve=
rything app. But he declined to elaborate, saying observers will just have =
to =E2=80=9Cstay tuned to find out.=E2=80=9D</p></blockquote>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you are a big law firm hired to work on a b=
ig case for a big company, your basic approach is going to be leaving no st=
one unturned. You will put a huge team on the case and pursue every possibl=
e avenue, even ones that contradict each other. You never know what will wo=
rk out, and you have lots of associates who can be staffed on the case, and=
 =E2=80=94=C2=A0this is crucial =E2=80=94=C2=A0<em>you bill by the hour</em=
>, so you try everything and see what sticks. Some effort will be wasted, o=
f course, but you can bill the client for all of it, and the client underst=
ands that in an important matter it is better to waste a lot of effort than=
 to miss something critical because you decided it wasn=E2=80=99t worth pur=
suing.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you are a big law firm hired to work on a b=
ig=C2=A0<em>bankruptcy</em>, there will be some constraints on this approac=
h, because your bills will be public and subject to court approval and, you=
 know, the client is bankrupt, so it can=E2=80=99t afford to be quite as la=
vish with your time as a non-bankrupt client would be. Still, the basic app=
roach is kind of in the DNA of big law firms, so it=E2=80=99s hard to resis=
t.</p>

<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Whether FTX=E2=80=99s new chief executive offi=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Attorneys for FTX have been exploring tax issu=

s=E2=80=9D for the exchange.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">John J. Ray III, the new CEO of FTX, told the =
Wall Street Journal in January that he=E2=80=99s=C2=A0exploring=C2=A0restar=
ting FTX.com, the company=E2=80=99s main international exchange, in a bid t=
o recoup value for creditors and customers. ...</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">According to a fee statement in February, Sull=
ivan & Cromwell lawyers worked on a variety of issues related to the ef=
fort. One note mentions analyzing =E2=80=9Cpotential security concerns re: =
potential FTX exchange reboot.=E2=80=9D Another mentions corresponding with=
 Sygnia Inc., the cybersecurity firm that advised FTX after the platform wa=
s hit by a post-bankruptcy hack in November, on potential reboot issues.</p=
>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Lawyers also corresponded with Ray on tax issu=
es. One fee was to =E2=80=9Canalyze possible reestablishment of exchange an=
d associated tax consequences, including US taxing jurisdiction.=E2=80=9D O=
ther tasks in February included email exchanges with Ray and other advisers=
 on the =E2=80=9Ccreation of mock-up exchange to test user experience.=E2=
=80=9D=C2=A0</p>
</blockquote>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But, look, fair enough, if I ran S&C=E2=80=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you buy some shares of a public company, an=

the CEO can=E2=80=99t just wire herself the company=E2=80=99s money, etc.),=
 but it is funny to imagine.</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Using a "clean account with a character with a=

sferred enough of his shares in the corporation to the infiltrator to enabl=

obody else in the corporation voted at all.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This was vital, because after 72 hours the two=
 "yes" votes carried the day. The infiltrating agent was very suddenly made=

ors and set to emptying the coffers.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">They stripped 130 billion ISK from the corpora=
te wallet, but that was only a small part of the haul: Counting all stolen =
assets, including multiple large ships, Flam_Hill estimated the total value=

</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I don=E2=80=99t know what =E2=80=9CISK=E2=80=

<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">ISK can't be legally cashed out of=
 EVE Online, but it can be used to buy Plex, an in-game currency used to up=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">[3] Also there is some sense in which you shou=
ldn=E2=80=99t have to trade, since after all the whole point of the wall-cr=
oss is that they are offering you the opportunity to invest in the SPAC dea=
l at $10 per share, so you don=E2=80=99t need to go insider trade by buying=
 stock in the market at $10 per share. You can make basically the same bet =
by just agreeing to the PIPE. Still you get more flexibility by buying in t=
he market: You can buy the stock now and dump it at a profit as soon as the=
 deal is announced, instead of waiting for the deal to close and being lock=
ed up for a while in the PIPE. Also a SPAC normally has warrants, publicly-=
traded options to buy its stock, and you can buy the warrants to make a lev=
eraged bet on the stock. Basically insider trading on a SPAC wall-cross is =
a quicker and cheaper way to bet on the SPAC than actually investing in the=
 PIPE. </p>
</div>
